title: Suspected Elder Abuse and Neglect: Recognition and Mandated Reporting
subtitle: An 82-year-old woman arrives from home with a Stage 4 sacral pressure injury, dehydration, malnutrition, urosepsis, and patterned bruises of different ages. The son's story doesn't fit the findings. The nurse must recognize the cues, keep her safe, and report.

patient.name: E. Calderón
patient.admitDate: Brought from home by EMS after a reported single fall. Found on the floor in a soiled brief with a large sacral wound, dehydrated and septic. Multiple findings inconsistent with the story raise concern for abuse and neglect.

labs.v: Grip marks, varied ages, atypical sites.
Phone APS within 24h; written within 2 days.
A capable adult may refuse intervention.
Describe findings; verbatim quotes; photos w/ consent.
The hospital is a safe haven.

nursesNote: E. Calderón, 82, arrives by EMS after her son reports a single fall, but the findings tell a different story: a chronic Stage 4 sacral pressure injury with exposed bone, severe dehydration and malnutrition (27% weight loss), untreated urosepsis, contractures, poor hygiene, and grip-mark bruises of different ages in atypical locations. This history-injury mismatch, plus neglect cues and patterned bruising, raises a reasonable suspicion of abuse and neglect — and the nurse, as a mandated reporter, needs only reasonable suspicion (not proof) to report. The nurse interviews the patient privately (son out of the room, professional interpreter — not a family member), and she discloses 'Sometimes he grabs me' while also saying 'I don't want him in trouble.' Findings are documented objectively and descriptively with verbatim quotes and consented photos, avoiding conclusory language. A mandated report is made to Adult Protective Services (patient consent is not required), the suspected abuser's bedside access is restricted under a safety plan, and the patient is not discharged to him. Although a capable adult may later refuse intervention, the report and safety plan still proceed, with social work, APS, and the supportive daughter coordinating care and post-acute placement.
providerOrders: Stabilize medically (treat urosepsis, dehydration, malnutrition, and wounds)
Interview the patient privately with a professional interpreter; document objectively
Report suspected abuse to APS on reasonable suspicion (no consent required)
Implement a safety plan; restrict the suspected abuser's access; do not discharge to him
Coordinate social work, APS, and supportive family for ongoing safety and placement

opts.text: A Stage 4 pressure injury, dehydration, and malnutrition suggest chronic neglect
Patterned bruises of different ages in atypical sites raise concern for physical abuse
A history that cannot explain the injuries (history-injury mismatch) is a key cue
Skin fragility and anticoagulants can mimic bruising and should be considered
Because the son explains it as one fall, no further concern is needed
The patient's statement that her caregiver 'grabs' her is a significant cue
The RN is a mandated reporter; reasonable suspicion (not proof) is the threshold
Patient consent is not required to make a mandated abuse report
The patient should be interviewed privately, never with the suspected abuser present
A capable adult may refuse intervention, but the report is still required
The nurse should investigate and prove abuse before reporting
Documentation should be objective and descriptive, not conclusory
Ensure immediate safety and medical stabilization
Interview the patient privately and document objective findings
Make the mandated report to APS
Implement a safety plan (restrict the suspected abuser's access)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care and post-acute planning
Report the suspected abuse to APS
Interview the patient with the son out of the room and an interpreter
Document findings objectively with photos taken with consent
Keep the patient safe and do not discharge her to the suspected abuser
Wait to report until abuse is proven
Confront and interrogate the son to get a confession
Ask the patient 'why did you let this happen?'
Use the son as the interpreter for the sensitive interview

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Treat the urosepsis, wounds, dehydration, and malnutrition
Conduct a private, trauma-informed interview and document verbatim
Phone APS and complete the written report
Restrict the suspected abuser's bedside access per the safety plan
Engage social work, APS, and the supportive daughter for placement
The nurse reports to APS on reasonable suspicion without waiting for proof
Findings are documented objectively with photos taken with consent
The patient is interviewed privately with a professional interpreter
The patient is kept in the hospital and not discharged to the suspected abuser
The nurse delays reporting until the investigation confirms abuse
The chart states 'patient was clearly battered by her son'
The son is allowed to stay and translate during the safety interview
The patient is discharged home with the son to avoid conflict
